summary: Gruber follows up on his PastryKit coverage, detailing Apple's multiple internal JavaScript frameworks and how PastryKit relates to TuneKit (about 40% shared code). He explores whether PastryKit is a leftover from pre-SDK days (concluding it's not, since it uses iPhone OS 3.0 features), and examines the strategic question of whether Apple should worry about web apps competing with the App Store. He argues Apple should embrace web apps as a cross-platform complement to native development, noting that desktop web apps haven't hurt Mac sales and that web apps could serve as a healthy alternative to the tightly-controlled App Store.
key_points: PastryKit and TuneKit share roughly 40% identical code (differing only in class name prefixes), suggesting they are sibling projects or the work of the same developer
PastryKit is definitively new, not a leftover from the pre-SDK era — it leverages iPhone OS 3.0 WebKit features and is far more sophisticated than earlier user guide implementations
WebKit-based browsers on iPhone, Android, and WebOS share the same rendering core, making cross-platform web apps technically feasible with far less effort than separate native apps
Apple's App Store exists primarily to sell hardware, not as a profit center in itself, so web apps shouldn't threaten Apple's core business
Web apps could serve as a healthy no-approval-necessary alternative to the App Store without diminishing native development
money_quotes: There's no question that the App Store exists to sell iPhones and iPod Touches, not the other way around.

I think Apple would do well to encourage web apps for cross-platform mobile software, and that doing so would not slow interest in native Cocoa Touch development for performance (especially games) and access to hardware (like the camera) not available to web apps.
I also think it would benefit both iPhone users and Apple itself to have no-approval-necessary web apps thrive as an alternative to the tightly-controlled App Store.
It's human nature to emphasize conflict and drama, but there's no reason why native iPhone apps and iPhone-optimized web apps can't both be thriving, growing platforms.
The Mac has thrived as the web has replaced Windows as the most widespread software platform.
key_insight: Apple should embrace iPhone web apps as a complementary cross-platform alternative to the App Store, since the App Store's purpose is selling hardware and web apps haven't hurt native platform adoption on the desktop.
